candidate_problems.text: How can 'counterfactual reach' be formalized so it is not merely a relabeling of existing notions like model-based planning, meta-learning, causal abstraction, or reflective agency? The unresolved tension is whether the proposed strange-loop/self-referential closure adds a genuinely new load-bearing constraint, or whether every apparent example can be reduced to fixed-ontology intervention over an expanded state space. A worthwhile problem is to derive necessary and sufficient conditions, plus discriminating examples and non-examples, that separate this conjecture from nearby concepts.

What objective measure could capture 'depth and coherence of recursively evaluable alternative-world generation' without collapsing into proxy metrics such as predictive accuracy, compression, search breadth, or robustness? The open question is whether there exists a non-arbitrary quantitative invariant of counterfactual reach that tracks policy-relevant representational self-revision while remaining comparable across systems. This is worth pursuing because without a measurement theory, the conjecture risks being evocative but unfalsifiable.

What are the coherence limits of self-referential counterfactual revision: when does allowing a system to counterfactually manipulate its own ontology preserve usable agency, and when does it produce regress, instability, or incoherent world-model fragmentation? The tension is that the conjecture requires reflective re-carving of variables, intervention targets, and agent boundaries, but such flexibility may undermine the very mutual interpretability and action-guidance the concept is supposed to enhance. A new problem is to characterize the structural conditions under which the loop is generative rather than destructive.
